ShopFast — E-commerce Platform

Generated: 2026-07-19 01:09 UTC Methodologies: STRIDE, LINDDUN, PASTA LLM-enhanced: No (not requested)

System Description

Reference e-commerce system: browser → edge → services → data tier, with an external payment processor.

Severity Overview

Info
288 total threats — 288 rule-based, 0 LLM-enhanced, 54 cross-boundary.

Data-flow overview

ShopFast — E-commerce Platform has 12 components and 11 data flows across 5 trust zones. Requests enter at Web App and API Gateway, data is stored in Orders DB, Sessions Cache, Event Bus and Object Store, it depends on external Stripe. 9 trust-boundary crossings, 1 unencrypted flow, the highest-risk component is Orders DB (4 critical findings).

Entry points: Web App, API GatewayData stores: Orders DB, Sessions Cache, Event Bus, Object StoreExternal deps: StripeBoundary crossings: 9Unencrypted flows: 1
FlowWhy it's riskySeverity
Catalog Service → Object Storeunencrypted; crosses a trust boundary; no/weak authenticationCritical
Customer → Web Appcrosses a trust boundaryCritical
API Gateway → Auth Servicecrosses a trust boundaryCritical
API Gateway → Orders Servicecrosses a trust boundaryCritical
API Gateway → Catalog Servicecrosses a trust boundaryCritical
Auth Service → Sessions Cachecrosses a trust boundaryCritical
Orders Service → Orders DBcrosses a trust boundaryCritical
Orders Service → Event Buscrosses a trust boundaryCritical
Risk hotspots: Orders DB (4C/18H) · Catalog Service (3C/18H) · Orders Service (3C/18H) · Web App (3C/18H)
⚠ Treat as unverified: 1 flow with no/weak authentication; 1 unencrypted flow.

🚧 Untrusted-Input Boundary Crossings

Flows where untrusted (or less-trusted) input crosses into an internal trust zone. These are the highest-priority validation points in the system — every byte that enters here must be treated as hostile until proven otherwise.

No untrusted-input boundary crossings detected. Either there are no internal zones defined, or no flows enter them from less-trusted zones.

🛡 Application Tier

4 components 3 ingress flows 5 egress flows 18 cross-boundary threats
Contains: Auth Service (auth_service), Orders Service (api), Payments Service (payment_service), Catalog Service (api)
⬇ Ingress (data entering this zone):
  • API Gateway → Auth Service (API Gateway → Auth Service) — HTTPS, auth: mtls, encrypted
  • API Gateway → Orders Service (API Gateway → Orders Service) — HTTPS, auth: bearer, encrypted
  • API Gateway → Catalog Service (API Gateway → Catalog Service) — HTTPS, auth: bearer, encrypted
⬆ Egress (data leaving this zone):
  • Auth Service → Sessions Cache (Auth Service → Sessions Cache) — TCP, auth: password, encrypted
  • Orders Service → Orders DB (Orders Service → Orders DB) — TCP, auth: credentials, encrypted
  • Orders Service → Event Bus (Orders Service → Event Bus) — AMQP, auth: mtls, encrypted
  • Payments Service → Stripe (Payments Service → Stripe) — HTTPS, auth: api_key, encrypted
  • Catalog Service → Object Store (Catalog Service → Object Store) — HTTPS, auth: none, UNENCRYPTED

🛡 Data Tier

4 components 4 ingress flows 0 egress flows 24 cross-boundary threats
Contains: Orders DB (database), Sessions Cache (cache), Event Bus (queue), Object Store (object_storage)
⬇ Ingress (data entering this zone):
  • Auth Service → Sessions Cache (Auth Service → Sessions Cache) — TCP, auth: password, encrypted
  • Orders Service → Orders DB (Orders Service → Orders DB) — TCP, auth: credentials, encrypted
  • Orders Service → Event Bus (Orders Service → Event Bus) — AMQP, auth: mtls, encrypted
  • Catalog Service → Object Store (Catalog Service → Object Store) — HTTPS, auth: none, UNENCRYPTED

Threats (288)

Showing 98 evidenced threats (proven by your model). 190 baseline type-based checks are hidden — reveal them with the “Baseline checks” toggle.

Broken access control / missing authz check
Critical STRIDE · Elevation of Privilege · Auth Service (auth_service) CWE-269
▼
CVSS 3.1 8.3
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:L
CVSS 4.0 6.3
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:H/VI:H/VA:L/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=10, R=10, E=10, A=10, D=10 · 50/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Auth Service (auth_service).

📝Description

Endpoints fail to verify the caller has permission for the action.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker authenticates to Auth Service as a low-privilege user, or reaches an unauthenticated entry point.
  2. Identifies an authorization gap — a function that doesn't re-check the caller's role, an IDOR-style URL, an admin endpoint with weak gating.
  3. Issues requests to that gap, gaining access to data or actions reserved for higher-privilege roles.
  4. Now operating with elevated privilege, attacker can pivot to other components, exfiltrate data, or persist access.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authorize on every action, not just at session start
Every privileged endpoint checks the current principal's roles/permissions for the specific resource being acted on. No 'logged in = allowed'.
preventive
Apply least-privilege to service identities
The IAM/role attached to Auth Service should grant only the actions it actually performs. Audit IAM grants quarterly.
detective
Detect anomalous privilege use
Alert when a principal performs an action they have not performed in the trailing 90 days, or when admin-tier actions originate from non-admin networks.

🔗References

A01:2021 — Broken Access Control ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-269 — Improper Privilege Management ↗

DREAD: D=10, R=8, E=10, A=10, D=10 · 48/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Auth Service (auth_service).

📝Description

Attacker breaks out of a sandbox to gain host privileges.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker authenticates to Auth Service as a low-privilege user, or reaches an unauthenticated entry point.
  2. Identifies an authorization gap — a function that doesn't re-check the caller's role, an IDOR-style URL, an admin endpoint with weak gating.
  3. Issues requests to that gap, gaining access to data or actions reserved for higher-privilege roles.
  4. Now operating with elevated privilege, attacker can pivot to other components, exfiltrate data, or persist access.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authorize on every action, not just at session start
Every privileged endpoint checks the current principal's roles/permissions for the specific resource being acted on. No 'logged in = allowed'.
preventive
Apply least-privilege to service identities
The IAM/role attached to Auth Service should grant only the actions it actually performs. Audit IAM grants quarterly.
detective
Detect anomalous privilege use
Alert when a principal performs an action they have not performed in the trailing 90 days, or when admin-tier actions originate from non-admin networks.

Critical STRIDE · Elevation of Privilege · Auth Service (auth_service) CWE-441 ⚡ cross-zone
▼

CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:H/A:L

CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:H/VI:H/VA:L/SC:H/SI:H/SA:L
DREAD: D=10, R=8, E=9, A=10, D=10 · 47/50
Boundary crossing: Edge → Application Tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow API Gateway → Auth Service (label: *API Gateway → Auth Service*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: mtls, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Edge into Application Tier. The receiving component is Auth Service (auth_service).

📝Description

If the receiver in 'Application Tier' acts on behalf of the caller, attackers compromising 'Edge' may inherit the receiver's privileges (confused-deputy).

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker compromises a low-privilege component in zone `Edge`.
  2. Issues requests to Auth Service that get re-executed with the receiver's higher privileges (classic confused-deputy).
  3. Because authorization is checked once at request entry but not per-action against the originating caller, the attacker effectively becomes a privileged actor in `Application Tier`.
  4. Attacker performs actions far beyond the original principal's permissions.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Use scoped, short-lived delegation tokens
When Auth Service acts on behalf of a caller, attach a delegation token with the caller's identity, scope (action allow-list), and ≤ 5-min TTL.
preventive
Authorize per-action against the originating caller
Don't rely on ambient authority of the receiver. Each action checks: (a) the delegation token is valid, (b) the original caller has permission for this specific action, (c) the action is within scope.
detective
Audit each privilege transit
Log: original caller → delegating component → action performed. Make this queryable for incident response.

🔗References

A01:2021 — Broken Access Control ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-441 — Confused Deputy ↗

Boundary crossing: Edge → Application Tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow API Gateway → Catalog Service (label: *API Gateway → Catalog Service*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: bearer, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Edge into Application Tier. The receiving component is Catalog Service (api).

📝Description

If the receiver in 'Application Tier' acts on behalf of the caller, attackers compromising 'Edge' may inherit the receiver's privileges (confused-deputy).

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker compromises a low-privilege component in zone `Edge`.
  2. Issues requests to Catalog Service that get re-executed with the receiver's higher privileges (classic confused-deputy).
  3. Because authorization is checked once at request entry but not per-action against the originating caller, the attacker effectively becomes a privileged actor in `Application Tier`.
  4. Attacker performs actions far beyond the original principal's permissions.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Use scoped, short-lived delegation tokens
When Catalog Service acts on behalf of a caller, attach a delegation token with the caller's identity, scope (action allow-list), and ≤ 5-min TTL.
preventive
Authorize per-action against the originating caller
Don't rely on ambient authority of the receiver. Each action checks: (a) the delegation token is valid, (b) the original caller has permission for this specific action, (c) the action is within scope.
detective
Audit each privilege transit
Log: original caller → delegating component → action performed. Make this queryable for incident response.

Stored data tampering via injection
Critical STRIDE · Tampering · Event Bus (queue) CWE-345
▼
CVSS 3.1 6.5
CVSS:3.1/AV:A/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:H/A:N
CVSS 4.0 5.2
CVSS:4.0/AV:A/AC:L/AT:N/PR:N/UI:N/VC:N/VI:H/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=9, R=10, E=9, A=7, D=9 · 44/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Event Bus (queue).

📝Description

SQL/NoSQL/command injection alters records or schema.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches an input vector exposed by Event Bus (request body, query string, message queue payload, persisted state).
  2. Submits malformed input designed to alter the program's logic or stored data (injection, parameter tampering, deserialization gadgets).
  3. Event Bus processes the input without strict validation, and the malicious change takes effect.
  4. Tampered data is persisted, executed, or relayed downstream — corrupting integrity beyond the original entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Schema-validate and parameterize all inputs
JSON Schema / OpenAPI validation at API edges; parameterized queries / prepared statements for SQL; ORM-level validators on persisted models.
preventive
Sign and verify integrity-critical messages
For commands or state mutations, attach an HMAC or signature so downstream consumers can detect tampering in transit or at rest.
detective
Use append-only / write-once stores for audit-critical data
Where data must not be silently changed, write to an append-only log (e.g., immutable S3 bucket, CloudTrail-like store) and reconcile against the mutable copy.

🔗References

A03:2021 — Injection ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-345 — Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity ↗

📝Description

If the receiver in 'Data Tier' acts on behalf of the caller, attackers compromising 'Application Tier' may inherit the receiver's privileges (confused-deputy).

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker compromises a low-privilege component in zone `Application Tier`.
  2. Issues requests to Event Bus that get re-executed with the receiver's higher privileges (classic confused-deputy).
  3. Because authorization is checked once at request entry but not per-action against the originating caller, the attacker effectively becomes a privileged actor in `Data Tier`.
  4. Attacker performs actions far beyond the original principal's permissions.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Use scoped, short-lived delegation tokens
When Event Bus acts on behalf of a caller, attach a delegation token with the caller's identity, scope (action allow-list), and ≤ 5-min TTL.
preventive
Authorize per-action against the originating caller
Don't rely on ambient authority of the receiver. Each action checks: (a) the delegation token is valid, (b) the original caller has permission for this specific action, (c) the action is within scope.
detective
Audit each privilege transit
Log: original caller → delegating component → action performed. Make this queryable for incident response.

  1. Attacker compromises a low-privilege component in zone `Application Tier`.
  2. Issues requests to Object Store that get re-executed with the receiver's higher privileges (classic confused-deputy).
  3. Because authorization is checked once at request entry but not per-action against the originating caller, the attacker effectively becomes a privileged actor in `Data Tier`.
  4. Attacker performs actions far beyond the original principal's permissions.

preventive
Use scoped, short-lived delegation tokens

preventive
Authorize per-action against the originating caller

preventive
Apply least-privilege to service identities
The IAM/role attached to Orders DB should grant only the actions it actually performs. Audit IAM grants quarterly.
detective
Detect anomalous privilege use
Alert when a principal performs an action they have not performed in the trailing 90 days, or when admin-tier actions originate from non-admin networks.

🔗References

A01:2021 — Broken Access Control ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-269 — Improper Privilege Management ↗
Privilege transit across boundary: Orders Service → Orders DB
Critical STRIDE · Elevation of Privilege · Orders DB (database) CWE-441 ⚡ cross-zone
▼
CVSS 3.1 8.9
CVSS:3.1/AV:A/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:H/A:L
CVSS 4.0 7.4
CVSS:4.0/AV:A/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:H/VI:H/VA:L/SC:H/SI:H/SA:L
DREAD: D=10, R=8, E=9, A=10, D=9 · 46/50
Boundary crossing: Application Tier → Data Tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Orders Service → Orders DB (label: *Orders Service → Orders DB*, protocol: TCP, auth: credentials, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Application Tier into Data Tier. The receiving component is Orders DB (database).

📝Description

If the receiver in 'Data Tier' acts on behalf of the caller, attackers compromising 'Application Tier' may inherit the receiver's privileges (confused-deputy).

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker compromises a low-privilege component in zone `Application Tier`.
  2. Issues requests to Orders DB that get re-executed with the receiver's higher privileges (classic confused-deputy).
  3. Because authorization is checked once at request entry but not per-action against the originating caller, the attacker effectively becomes a privileged actor in `Data Tier`.
  4. Attacker performs actions far beyond the original principal's permissions.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Use scoped, short-lived delegation tokens
When Orders DB acts on behalf of a caller, attach a delegation token with the caller's identity, scope (action allow-list), and ≤ 5-min TTL.
preventive
Authorize per-action against the originating caller
Don't rely on ambient authority of the receiver. Each action checks: (a) the delegation token is valid, (b) the original caller has permission for this specific action, (c) the action is within scope.
detective
Audit each privilege transit
Log: original caller → delegating component → action performed. Make this queryable for incident response.

🔗References

A01:2021 — Broken Access Control ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-441 — Confused Deputy ↗
Broken access control / missing authz check
Critical STRIDE · Elevation of Privilege · Orders Service (api) CWE-269
▼
CVSS 3.1 8.3
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:L
CVSS 4.0 6.3
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:H/VI:H/VA:L/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=9, R=10, E=10, A=10, D=10 · 49/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Orders Service (api).

📝Description

Endpoints fail to verify the caller has permission for the action.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker authenticates to Orders Service as a low-privilege user, or reaches an unauthenticated entry point.
  2. Identifies an authorization gap — a function that doesn't re-check the caller's role, an IDOR-style URL, an admin endpoint with weak gating.
  3. Issues requests to that gap, gaining access to data or actions reserved for higher-privilege roles.
  4. Now operating with elevated privilege, attacker can pivot to other components, exfiltrate data, or persist access.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authorize on every action, not just at session start
Every privileged endpoint checks the current principal's roles/permissions for the specific resource being acted on. No 'logged in = allowed'.
preventive
Apply least-privilege to service identities
The IAM/role attached to Orders Service should grant only the actions it actually performs. Audit IAM grants quarterly.
detective
Detect anomalous privilege use
Alert when a principal performs an action they have not performed in the trailing 90 days, or when admin-tier actions originate from non-admin networks.

🔗References

📝Description

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker authenticates to Orders Service as a low-privilege user, or reaches an unauthenticated entry point.
  2. Identifies an authorization gap — a function that doesn't re-check the caller's role, an IDOR-style URL, an admin endpoint with weak gating.
  3. Issues requests to that gap, gaining access to data or actions reserved for higher-privilege roles.
  4. Now operating with elevated privilege, attacker can pivot to other components, exfiltrate data, or persist access.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authorize on every action, not just at session start
Every privileged endpoint checks the current principal's roles/permissions for the specific resource being acted on. No 'logged in = allowed'.

Apply least-privilege to service identities
The IAM/role attached to Orders Service should grant only the actions it actually performs. Audit IAM grants quarterly.
detective
Detect anomalous privilege use

Boundary crossing: Edge → Application Tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow API Gateway → Orders Service (label: *API Gateway → Orders Service*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: bearer, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Edge into Application Tier. The receiving component is Orders Service (api).

📝Description

If the receiver in 'Application Tier' acts on behalf of the caller, attackers compromising 'Edge' may inherit the receiver's privileges (confused-deputy).

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker compromises a low-privilege component in zone `Edge`.
  2. Issues requests to Orders Service that get re-executed with the receiver's higher privileges (classic confused-deputy).
  3. Because authorization is checked once at request entry but not per-action against the originating caller, the attacker effectively becomes a privileged actor in `Application Tier`.
  4. Attacker performs actions far beyond the original principal's permissions.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Use scoped, short-lived delegation tokens
When Orders Service acts on behalf of a caller, attach a delegation token with the caller's identity, scope (action allow-list), and ≤ 5-min TTL.
preventive
Authorize per-action against the originating caller
Don't rely on ambient authority of the receiver. Each action checks: (a) the delegation token is valid, (b) the original caller has permission for this specific action, (c) the action is within scope.
detective
Audit each privilege transit
Log: original caller → delegating component → action performed. Make this queryable for incident response.

A01:2021 — Broken Access Control ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-441 — Confused Deputy ↗
Stored data tampering via injection
Critical STRIDE · Tampering · Sessions Cache (cache) CWE-345
▼
CVSS 3.1 6.5
CVSS:3.1/AV:A/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:H/A:N
CVSS 4.0 5.2
CVSS:4.0/AV:A/AC:L/AT:N/PR:N/UI:N/VC:N/VI:H/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=9, R=10, E=9, A=7, D=9 · 44/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Sessions Cache (cache).

📝Description

SQL/NoSQL/command injection alters records or schema.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches an input vector exposed by Sessions Cache (request body, query string, message queue payload, persisted state).
  2. Submits malformed input designed to alter the program's logic or stored data (injection, parameter tampering, deserialization gadgets).
  3. Sessions Cache processes the input without strict validation, and the malicious change takes effect.
  4. Tampered data is persisted, executed, or relayed downstream — corrupting integrity beyond the original entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Schema-validate and parameterize all inputs
JSON Schema / OpenAPI validation at API edges; parameterized queries / prepared statements for SQL; ORM-level validators on persisted models.
preventive
Sign and verify integrity-critical messages
For commands or state mutations, attach an HMAC or signature so downstream consumers can detect tampering in transit or at rest.
detective
Use append-only / write-once stores for audit-critical data
Where data must not be silently changed, write to an append-only log (e.g., immutable S3 bucket, CloudTrail-like store) and reconcile against the mutable copy.

🔗References

A03:2021 — Injection ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-345 — Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity ↗

📝Description

  3. Because authorization is checked once at request entry but not per-action against the originating caller, the attacker effectively becomes a privileged actor in `Data Tier`.
  4. Attacker performs actions far beyond the original principal's permissions.

Use scoped, short-lived delegation tokens
When Sessions Cache acts on behalf of a caller, attach a delegation token with the caller's identity, scope (action allow-list), and ≤ 5-min TTL.

Don't rely on ambient authority of the receiver. Each action checks: (a) the delegation token is valid, (b) the original caller has permission for this specific action, (c) the action is within scope.
detective

Log: original caller → delegating component → action performed. Make this queryable for incident response.

  4. Attacker performs actions far beyond the original principal's permissions.

Boundary crossing: Public Internet → Edge

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Customer → Web App (label: *Customer → Web App*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: session, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Public Internet into Edge. The receiving component is Web App (webapp).

📝Description

If the receiver in 'Edge' acts on behalf of the caller, attackers compromising 'Public Internet' may inherit the receiver's privileges (confused-deputy).

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker compromises a low-privilege component in zone `Public Internet`.
  2. Issues requests to Web App that get re-executed with the receiver's higher privileges (classic confused-deputy).
  3. Because authorization is checked once at request entry but not per-action against the originating caller, the attacker effectively becomes a privileged actor in `Edge`.
  4. Attacker performs actions far beyond the original principal's permissions.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Use scoped, short-lived delegation tokens
When Web App acts on behalf of a caller, attach a delegation token with the caller's identity, scope (action allow-list), and ≤ 5-min TTL.
preventive
Authorize per-action against the originating caller
Don't rely on ambient authority of the receiver. Each action checks: (a) the delegation token is valid, (b) the original caller has permission for this specific action, (c) the action is within scope.
detective
Audit each privilege transit
Log: original caller → delegating component → action performed. Make this queryable for incident response.

A01:2021 — Broken Access Control ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-441 — Confused Deputy ↗
Authentication bypass via credential stuffing
High STRIDE · Spoofing · Auth Service (auth_service) CWE-287
▼
CVSS 3.1 7.5
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:N
CVSS 4.0 7.0
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:N/UI:N/VC:H/VI:N/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=8, R=6, E=9, A=8, D=9 · 40/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Auth Service (auth_service).

📝Description

Attackers reuse leaked credentials to impersonate legitimate users.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker locates Auth Service's identity claim mechanism (token format, header name, signing key handling).
  2. Forges or replays a token to assert another principal's identity.
  3. Auth Service accepts the claim because verification is missing or weak (e.g., signature not checked, expired tokens accepted, no audience validation).
  4. Attacker acts as the spoofed principal — reading data, triggering workflows, or pivoting to internal services.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate token signatures with explicit algorithm allow-listing
Reject 'alg: none', reject HS256 when expecting RS256. Use a vetted JWT library; never hand-roll verification.
preventive
Enforce audience and issuer claims
Every token must declare which service it's intended for. Reject tokens whose audience doesn't match this component.
preventive
Bind tokens to a session or device
Use mTLS-bound tokens, DPoP, or token binding to prevent replay if a token is captured.

🔗References

A07:2021 — Identification and Authentication Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-287 — Improper Authentication ↗
Session token hijacking
High STRIDE · Spoofing · Auth Service (auth_service) CWE-287

CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:N/UI:N/VC:H/VI:N/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=8, R=6, E=9, A=8, D=9 · 40/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Auth Service (auth_service).

📝Description

Attacker steals or predicts a session token to impersonate a user.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker locates Auth Service's identity claim mechanism (token format, header name, signing key handling).
  2. Forges or replays a token to assert another principal's identity.
  3. Auth Service accepts the claim because verification is missing or weak (e.g., signature not checked, expired tokens accepted, no audience validation).
  4. Attacker acts as the spoofed principal — reading data, triggering workflows, or pivoting to internal services.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate token signatures with explicit algorithm allow-listing
Reject 'alg: none', reject HS256 when expecting RS256. Use a vetted JWT library; never hand-roll verification.
preventive
Enforce audience and issuer claims
Every token must declare which service it's intended for. Reject tokens whose audience doesn't match this component.
preventive
Bind tokens to a session or device
Use mTLS-bound tokens, DPoP, or token binding to prevent replay if a token is captured.

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Auth Service (auth_service).

📝Description

An attacker obtains or forges an API key to act as a trusted service.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker locates Auth Service's identity claim mechanism (token format, header name, signing key handling).
  2. Forges or replays a token to assert another principal's identity.
  3. Auth Service accepts the claim because verification is missing or weak (e.g., signature not checked, expired tokens accepted, no audience validation).
  4. Attacker acts as the spoofed principal — reading data, triggering workflows, or pivoting to internal services.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate token signatures with explicit algorithm allow-listing
Reject 'alg: none', reject HS256 when expecting RS256. Use a vetted JWT library; never hand-roll verification.
preventive
Enforce audience and issuer claims
Every token must declare which service it's intended for. Reject tokens whose audience doesn't match this component.
preventive
Bind tokens to a session or device
Use mTLS-bound tokens, DPoP, or token binding to prevent replay if a token is captured.

🔗References

A07:2021 — Identification and Authentication Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-287 — Improper Authentication ↗
Log tampering
High STRIDE · Repudiation · Auth Service (auth_service) CWE-778
▼
CVSS 3.1 9.1
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:N
CVSS 4.0 7.0
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:N/UI:N/VC:L/VI:H/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=8, R=6, E=9, A=8, D=9 · 40/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Auth Service (auth_service).

📝Description

An attacker with access modifies or deletes audit trails.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. User performs a sensitive action through Auth Service (a transfer, a permission change, a deletion).
  2. Audit logs are absent, incomplete, or modifiable by the same principal who performed the action.
  3. User later denies having performed the action, or an attacker covers their tracks.
  4. Without tamper-evident logs, neither responsible party can be identified — leading to fraud, dispute, or regulatory exposure.

🛡How to mitigate

detective
Emit tamper-evident audit logs
Sign log entries (HMAC chain, hash-linked) so insertion or deletion is detectable. Forward to a separate, append-only system the action's principal cannot administer.
detective
Capture sufficient detail per audit event
Who (authenticated principal, not just session ID), what (specific action and target), when (UTC, monotonic-clock-corroborated), where (source IP, request ID), why (correlated to the upstream business event).
detective
Periodic log integrity verification
Schedule daily integrity checks on the audit log chain. Alert on any gap.

🔗References

A09:2021 — Security Logging and Monitoring Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-778 — Insufficient Logging ↗
Application-layer DDoS
High STRIDE · Denial of Service · Auth Service (auth_service) CWE-400
▼
CVSS 3.1 9.1
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:H
CVSS 4.0 7.0
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:N/UI:N/VC:L/VI:N/VA:H/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=8, R=6, E=9, A=8, D=9 · 40/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Auth Service (auth_service).

📝Description

Attacker floods expensive endpoints (e.g., search, login).

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker identifies a costly operation in Auth Service (regex matching, file generation, expensive query, large allocation).
  2. Issues many concurrent requests targeting that operation, or a single request with pathological input.
  3. Auth Service's resources (CPU, memory, connections, disk) saturate or exhaust.
  4. Legitimate users can no longer reach Auth Service; cascading failure may take down dependents.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply input limits at the edge
Maximum request size, body depth, array length, and string length. Reject early — before parsing or business logic runs.
preventive
Rate-limit per principal and per resource
Token bucket per authenticated user AND per costly endpoint. Adaptive throttling on saturation.
corrective
Set hard timeouts on outbound calls
No call (DB, downstream service, third-party API) should be able to hang the request handler indefinitely. Use circuit breakers and bulkheads.

🔗References

A05:2021 — Security Misconfiguration ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-400 — Uncontrolled Resource Consumption (DoS) ↗
Privilege escalation via mass assignment
High STRIDE · Elevation of Privilege · Auth Service (auth_service) CWE-269
▼
CVSS 3.1 8.3
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:L
CVSS 4.0 6.3
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:H/VI:H/VA:L/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=8, R=8, E=9, A=8, D=9 · 42/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Auth Service (auth_service).

📝Description

User submits extra fields (e.g., role=admin) and they bind to the model.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker authenticates to Auth Service as a low-privilege user, or reaches an unauthenticated entry point.
  2. Identifies an authorization gap — a function that doesn't re-check the caller's role, an IDOR-style URL, an admin endpoint with weak gating.
  3. Issues requests to that gap, gaining access to data or actions reserved for higher-privilege roles.
  4. Now operating with elevated privilege, attacker can pivot to other components, exfiltrate data, or persist access.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authorize on every action, not just at session start
Every privileged endpoint checks the current principal's roles/permissions for the specific resource being acted on. No 'logged in = allowed'.
preventive
Apply least-privilege to service identities
The IAM/role attached to Auth Service should grant only the actions it actually performs. Audit IAM grants quarterly.
detective
Detect anomalous privilege use
Alert when a principal performs an action they have not performed in the trailing 90 days, or when admin-tier actions originate from non-admin networks.

🔗References

A01:2021 — Broken Access Control ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-269 — Improper Privilege Management ↗
Trust-boundary crossing without strong authn: API Gateway → Auth Service
High STRIDE · Spoofing · Auth Service (auth_service) CWE-287 ⚡ cross-zone
▼
CVSS 3.1 7.7
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:N/A:N
CVSS 4.0 5.8
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:H/VI:N/VA:N/SC:H/SI:L/SA:N
DREAD: D=8, R=6, E=7, A=10, D=9 · 40/50
Boundary crossing: Edge → Application Tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow API Gateway → Auth Service (label: *API Gateway → Auth Service*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: mtls, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Edge into Application Tier. The receiving component is Auth Service (auth_service).

📝Description

Flow 'API Gateway → Auth Service' crosses trust boundary 'Edge' → 'Application Tier'. Caller identity must be re-verified at the boundary; existing trust does not transit.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker positions themselves on the network path between source and destination, or compromises an upstream component in the source zone.
  2. Crafts requests with forged or replayed identity claims (cookies, tokens, IP-based trust).
  3. Auth Service accepts the request because identity is not re-verified at the boundary, treating the upstream zone as trusted.
  4. Attacker can now perform actions as the impersonated principal across the boundary.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authenticate at the boundary
Require a fresh, audience-bound credential at the receiver. Do not infer identity from network position, source IP, or upstream session.
preventive
Use mutual TLS or signed tokens with audience claims
For service-to-service calls into Auth Service, terminate mTLS at the boundary and reject any caller without a valid client cert. For human-driven flows, use OAuth2 / OIDC with audience and issuer validation.
detective
Log every cross-boundary auth decision
Emit an authentication event (success and failure) tagged with both source and destination zone. Forward to SIEM with retention ≥ 90 days.

🔗References

A07:2021 — Identification and Authentication Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-287 — Improper Authentication ↗
Cross-boundary input not validated: API Gateway → Auth Service
High STRIDE · Tampering · Auth Service (auth_service) CWE-20 ⚡ cross-zone
▼
CVSS 3.1 9.6
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:H/A:N
CVSS 4.0 5.8
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:L/VI:H/VA:N/SC:L/SI:H/SA:N
DREAD: D=8, R=6, E=7, A=10, D=9 · 40/50
Boundary crossing: Edge → Application Tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow API Gateway → Auth Service (label: *API Gateway → Auth Service*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: mtls, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Edge into Application Tier. The receiving component is Auth Service (auth_service).

📝Description

Data crossing the trust boundary into 'Application Tier' must be treated as untrusted, even if the source is internal. Implicit trust is the most common cause of injection / SSRF / deserialization bugs.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker compromises a less-trusted upstream zone or directly sends malformed input to Auth Service.
  2. Sends payloads with unexpected types, lengths, or special characters (e.g. SQL meta-chars, NUL bytes, control sequences).
  3. Auth Service processes the input under the assumption that the upstream zone has already validated it — but the boundary changes the security context.
  4. Injection, deserialization flaws, or buffer issues trigger; attacker pivots into Auth Service or the zone behind it.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate against an allow-list schema at the boundary
Define an explicit schema (JSON Schema, Protobuf, OpenAPI) for every accepted message at this boundary. Reject anything that doesn't match — type, length, charset, enum values, nested depth.
preventive
Canonicalize before validation
Decode URL-encoding, Unicode normalization (NFC), trim whitespace, and resolve relative paths before validating. Avoid double-decoding bypasses.
preventive
Apply context-specific output encoding
Whatever Auth Service does with the input — SQL: parameterized queries, HTML: contextual escaping, OS commands: avoid shell, use exec arrays, LDAP: escape filter chars.

🔗References

A03:2021 — Injection ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-20 — Improper Input Validation ↗
Cross-boundary data exposure risk: API Gateway → Auth Service
High STRIDE · Information Disclosure · Auth Service (auth_service) CWE-200 ⚡ cross-zone
▼
CVSS 3.1 7.7
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:N/A:N
CVSS 4.0 5.8
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:H/VI:N/VA:N/SC:H/SI:L/SA:N
DREAD: D=8, R=6, E=7, A=10, D=10 · 41/50
Boundary crossing: Edge → Application Tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow API Gateway → Auth Service (label: *API Gateway → Auth Service*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: mtls, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Edge into Application Tier. The receiving component is Auth Service (auth_service).

📝Description

Information leaving 'Edge' into 'Application Tier' may include data the receiving zone is not authorized to see. Cross-boundary egress is a common data-leak surface.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker observes traffic leaving the source zone, or compromises a component in the destination zone.
  2. The destination zone receives more data than it strictly needs (over-fetching, verbose error responses, full record dumps).
  3. Attacker harvests sensitive fields (PII, secrets, internal IDs) that should never have left the source zone.
  4. Data is exfiltrated, sold, or used to plan a deeper attack on the originating zone.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Enforce minimum-data-needed at the egress point
Whitelist exactly which fields leave the source zone. Strip everything else server-side before serialization.
preventive
Tokenize or redact sensitive fields
Replace PII / PCI / PHI fields with reversible tokens or one-way hashes when the destination zone doesn't need the cleartext value.
detective
Log cross-boundary data flows for review
Sample-log the field set crossing this boundary (not the values) so DLP and privacy reviews can audit what's egressing.

🔗References

A02:2021 — Cryptographic Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
Authentication bypass via credential stuffing
High STRIDE · Spoofing · Catalog Service (api) CWE-287
▼
CVSS 3.1 7.5
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:N
CVSS 4.0 7.0
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:N/UI:N/VC:H/VI:N/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=7, R=6, E=9, A=8, D=9 · 39/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Catalog Service (api).

📝Description

Attackers reuse leaked credentials to impersonate legitimate users.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker locates Catalog Service's identity claim mechanism (token format, header name, signing key handling).
  2. Forges or replays a token to assert another principal's identity.
  3. Catalog Service accepts the claim because verification is missing or weak (e.g., signature not checked, expired tokens accepted, no audience validation).
  4. Attacker acts as the spoofed principal — reading data, triggering workflows, or pivoting to internal services.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate token signatures with explicit algorithm allow-listing
Reject 'alg: none', reject HS256 when expecting RS256. Use a vetted JWT library; never hand-roll verification.
preventive
Enforce audience and issuer claims
Every token must declare which service it's intended for. Reject tokens whose audience doesn't match this component.
preventive
Bind tokens to a session or device
Use mTLS-bound tokens, DPoP, or token binding to prevent replay if a token is captured.

🔗References

A07:2021 — Identification and Authentication Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-287 — Improper Authentication ↗
Session token hijacking
High STRIDE · Spoofing · Catalog Service (api) CWE-287

CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:N
CVSS 4.0 7.0
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:N/UI:N/VC:H/VI:N/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=7, R=6, E=9, A=8, D=9 · 39/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Catalog Service (api).

📝Description

Attacker steals or predicts a session token to impersonate a user.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker locates Catalog Service's identity claim mechanism (token format, header name, signing key handling).
  2. Forges or replays a token to assert another principal's identity.
  3. Catalog Service accepts the claim because verification is missing or weak (e.g., signature not checked, expired tokens accepted, no audience validation).
  4. Attacker acts as the spoofed principal — reading data, triggering workflows, or pivoting to internal services.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate token signatures with explicit algorithm allow-listing
Reject 'alg: none', reject HS256 when expecting RS256. Use a vetted JWT library; never hand-roll verification.
preventive
Enforce audience and issuer claims
Every token must declare which service it's intended for. Reject tokens whose audience doesn't match this component.
preventive
Bind tokens to a session or device
Use mTLS-bound tokens, DPoP, or token binding to prevent replay if a token is captured.

CVSS 4.0 7.0
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:N/UI:N/VC:H/VI:N/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=7, R=6, E=9, A=8, D=9 · 39/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Catalog Service (api).

📝Description

An attacker obtains or forges an API key to act as a trusted service.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker locates Catalog Service's identity claim mechanism (token format, header name, signing key handling).
  2. Forges or replays a token to assert another principal's identity.
  3. Catalog Service accepts the claim because verification is missing or weak (e.g., signature not checked, expired tokens accepted, no audience validation).
  4. Attacker acts as the spoofed principal — reading data, triggering workflows, or pivoting to internal services.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate token signatures with explicit algorithm allow-listing
Reject 'alg: none', reject HS256 when expecting RS256. Use a vetted JWT library; never hand-roll verification.
preventive
Enforce audience and issuer claims
Every token must declare which service it's intended for. Reject tokens whose audience doesn't match this component.
preventive
Bind tokens to a session or device
Use mTLS-bound tokens, DPoP, or token binding to prevent replay if a token is captured.

A07:2021 — Identification and Authentication Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-287 — Improper Authentication ↗
Log tampering
High STRIDE · Repudiation · Catalog Service (api) CWE-778
▼
CVSS 3.1 7.5
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:H/A:N
CVSS 4.0 7.0
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:N/UI:N/VC:N/VI:H/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=7, R=6, E=9, A=8, D=9 · 39/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Catalog Service (api).

📝Description

An attacker with access modifies or deletes audit trails.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. User performs a sensitive action through Catalog Service (a transfer, a permission change, a deletion).
  2. Audit logs are absent, incomplete, or modifiable by the same principal who performed the action.
  3. User later denies having performed the action, or an attacker covers their tracks.
  4. Without tamper-evident logs, neither responsible party can be identified — leading to fraud, dispute, or regulatory exposure.

🛡How to mitigate

detective
Emit tamper-evident audit logs
Sign log entries (HMAC chain, hash-linked) so insertion or deletion is detectable. Forward to a separate, append-only system the action's principal cannot administer.
detective
Capture sufficient detail per audit event
Who (authenticated principal, not just session ID), what (specific action and target), when (UTC, monotonic-clock-corroborated), where (source IP, request ID), why (correlated to the upstream business event).
detective
Periodic log integrity verification
Schedule daily integrity checks on the audit log chain. Alert on any gap.

🔗References

A09:2021 — Security Logging and Monitoring Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-778 — Insufficient Logging ↗
Sensitive data exposure in transit
High STRIDE · Information Disclosure · Catalog Service (api) CWE-319
▼
CVSS 3.1 7.5
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:N
CVSS 4.0 7.0
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:N/UI:N/VC:H/VI:N/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=7, R=6, E=9, A=8, D=10 · 40/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Catalog Service (api).

📝Description

PII, secrets, or tokens transmitted over unencrypted channels.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Catalog Service via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For api, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🔗References

A02:2021 — Cryptographic Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-319 — Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information ↗

  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For api, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Catalog Service via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.

Encrypt data at rest with key separation

Application-layer DDoS
High STRIDE · Denial of Service · Catalog Service (api) CWE-400
▼
CVSS 3.1 7.5
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:N/A:H
CVSS 4.0 7.0
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:N/UI:N/VC:N/VI:N/VA:H/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=7, R=6, E=9, A=8, D=9 · 39/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Catalog Service (api).

📝Description

Attacker floods expensive endpoints (e.g., search, login).

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker identifies a costly operation in Catalog Service (regex matching, file generation, expensive query, large allocation).
  2. Issues many concurrent requests targeting that operation, or a single request with pathological input.
  3. Catalog Service's resources (CPU, memory, connections, disk) saturate or exhaust.
  4. Legitimate users can no longer reach Catalog Service; cascading failure may take down dependents.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply input limits at the edge
Maximum request size, body depth, array length, and string length. Reject early — before parsing or business logic runs.
preventive
Rate-limit per principal and per resource
Token bucket per authenticated user AND per costly endpoint. Adaptive throttling on saturation.
corrective
Set hard timeouts on outbound calls
No call (DB, downstream service, third-party API) should be able to hang the request handler indefinitely. Use circuit breakers and bulkheads.

On the data flow API Gateway → Catalog Service (label: *API Gateway → Catalog Service*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: bearer, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Edge into Application Tier. The receiving component is Catalog Service (api).

📝Description

Flow 'API Gateway → Catalog Service' crosses trust boundary 'Edge' → 'Application Tier'. Caller identity must be re-verified at the boundary; existing trust does not transit.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker positions themselves on the network path between source and destination, or compromises an upstream component in the source zone.

  1. Attacker compromises a less-trusted upstream zone or directly sends malformed input to Catalog Service.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate against an allow-list schema at the boundary
Define an explicit schema (JSON Schema, Protobuf, OpenAPI) for every accepted message at this boundary. Reject anything that doesn't match — type, length, charset, enum values, nested depth.
preventive
Canonicalize before validation
Decode URL-encoding, Unicode normalization (NFC), trim whitespace, and resolve relative paths before validating. Avoid double-decoding bypasses.
preventive
Apply context-specific output encoding
Whatever Catalog Service does with the input — SQL: parameterized queries, HTML: contextual escaping, OS commands: avoid shell, use exec arrays, LDAP: escape filter chars.

🔗References

A03:2021 — Injection ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-20 — Improper Input Validation ↗
Cross-boundary data exposure risk: API Gateway → Catalog Service
High STRIDE · Information Disclosure · Catalog Service (api) CWE-200 ⚡ cross-zone
▼
CVSS 3.1 7.7
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:N/A:N
CVSS 4.0 5.8
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:H/VI:N/VA:N/SC:H/SI:L/SA:N
DREAD: D=7, R=6, E=9, A=10, D=10 · 42/50
Boundary crossing: Edge → Application Tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow API Gateway → Catalog Service (label: *API Gateway → Catalog Service*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: bearer, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Edge into Application Tier. The receiving component is Catalog Service (api).

📝Description

Information leaving 'Edge' into 'Application Tier' may include data the receiving zone is not authorized to see. Cross-boundary egress is a common data-leak surface.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker observes traffic leaving the source zone, or compromises a component in the destination zone.
  2. The destination zone receives more data than it strictly needs (over-fetching, verbose error responses, full record dumps).
  3. Attacker harvests sensitive fields (PII, secrets, internal IDs) that should never have left the source zone.
  4. Data is exfiltrated, sold, or used to plan a deeper attack on the originating zone.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Enforce minimum-data-needed at the egress point
Whitelist exactly which fields leave the source zone. Strip everything else server-side before serialization.
preventive
Tokenize or redact sensitive fields
Replace PII / PCI / PHI fields with reversible tokens or one-way hashes when the destination zone doesn't need the cleartext value.
detective
Log cross-boundary data flows for review
Sample-log the field set crossing this boundary (not the values) so DLP and privacy reviews can audit what's egressing.

🔗References

A02:2021 — Cryptographic Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
Authentication bypass via credential stuffing
High STRIDE · Spoofing · Customer (user) CWE-287
▼
CVSS 3.1 7.5
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:N
CVSS 4.0 7.0
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:N/UI:N/VC:H/VI:N/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=7, R=6, E=9, A=5, D=9 · 36/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Customer (user).

📝Description

Attackers reuse leaked credentials to impersonate legitimate users.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker obtains valid credentials via phishing, credential stuffing, or a third-party breach.
  2. Logs in as the legitimate user/entity at Customer.
  3. Performs actions indistinguishable from the real user (auth events look normal).
  4. Damage scales with the impersonated user's permissions; admin accounts cause the most harm.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate token signatures with explicit algorithm allow-listing
Reject 'alg: none', reject HS256 when expecting RS256. Use a vetted JWT library; never hand-roll verification.
preventive
Enforce audience and issuer claims
Every token must declare which service it's intended for. Reject tokens whose audience doesn't match this component.
preventive
Bind tokens to a session or device
Use mTLS-bound tokens, DPoP, or token binding to prevent replay if a token is captured.

🔗References

A07:2021 — Identification and Authentication Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-287 — Improper Authentication ↗

CVSS 3.1 7.5
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:N
CVSS 4.0 7.0
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:N/UI:N/VC:H/VI:N/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=7, R=6, E=9, A=5, D=9 · 36/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Customer (user).

📝Description

Attacker steals or predicts a session token to impersonate a user.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker obtains valid credentials via phishing, credential stuffing, or a third-party breach.
  2. Logs in as the legitimate user/entity at Customer.
  3. Performs actions indistinguishable from the real user (auth events look normal).
  4. Damage scales with the impersonated user's permissions; admin accounts cause the most harm.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate token signatures with explicit algorithm allow-listing
Reject 'alg: none', reject HS256 when expecting RS256. Use a vetted JWT library; never hand-roll verification.
preventive
Enforce audience and issuer claims
Every token must declare which service it's intended for. Reject tokens whose audience doesn't match this component.
preventive
Bind tokens to a session or device
Use mTLS-bound tokens, DPoP, or token binding to prevent replay if a token is captured.

DREAD: D=7, R=6, E=9, A=5, D=9 · 36/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Customer (user).

📝Description

An attacker obtains or forges an API key to act as a trusted service.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker obtains valid credentials via phishing, credential stuffing, or a third-party breach.
  2. Logs in as the legitimate user/entity at Customer.
  3. Performs actions indistinguishable from the real user (auth events look normal).
  4. Damage scales with the impersonated user's permissions; admin accounts cause the most harm.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate token signatures with explicit algorithm allow-listing
Reject 'alg: none', reject HS256 when expecting RS256. Use a vetted JWT library; never hand-roll verification.
preventive
Enforce audience and issuer claims
Every token must declare which service it's intended for. Reject tokens whose audience doesn't match this component.
preventive
Bind tokens to a session or device
Use mTLS-bound tokens, DPoP, or token binding to prevent replay if a token is captured.

🔗References

A07:2021 — Identification and Authentication Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-287 — Improper Authentication ↗
Data-in-transit modification
High STRIDE · Tampering · Event Bus (queue) CWE-345
▼
CVSS 3.1 6.5
CVSS:3.1/AV:A/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:H/A:N
CVSS 4.0 5.2
CVSS:4.0/AV:A/AC:L/AT:N/PR:N/UI:N/VC:N/VI:H/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=7, R=6, E=7, A=5, D=7 · 32/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Event Bus (queue).

📝Description

Attacker on the network path alters request/response payloads.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches an input vector exposed by Event Bus (request body, query string, message queue payload, persisted state).
  2. Submits malformed input designed to alter the program's logic or stored data (injection, parameter tampering, deserialization gadgets).
  3. Event Bus processes the input without strict validation, and the malicious change takes effect.
  4. Tampered data is persisted, executed, or relayed downstream — corrupting integrity beyond the original entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Schema-validate and parameterize all inputs
JSON Schema / OpenAPI validation at API edges; parameterized queries / prepared statements for SQL; ORM-level validators on persisted models.
preventive
Sign and verify integrity-critical messages
For commands or state mutations, attach an HMAC or signature so downstream consumers can detect tampering in transit or at rest.
detective
Use append-only / write-once stores for audit-critical data
Where data must not be silently changed, write to an append-only log (e.g., immutable S3 bucket, CloudTrail-like store) and reconcile against the mutable copy.

🔗References

A03:2021 — Injection ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-345 — Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity ↗
Config / secret tampering
High STRIDE · Tampering · Event Bus (queue) CWE-345
▼
CVSS 3.1 6.5
CVSS:3.1/AV:A/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:H/A:N
CVSS 4.0 5.2
CVSS:4.0/AV:A/AC:L/AT:N/PR:N/UI:N/VC:N/VI:H/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=7, R=6, E=7, A=5, D=7 · 32/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Event Bus (queue).

📝Description

Unauthorized changes to runtime config alter security behavior.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches an input vector exposed by Event Bus (request body, query string, message queue payload, persisted state).
  2. Submits malformed input designed to alter the program's logic or stored data (injection, parameter tampering, deserialization gadgets).
  3. Event Bus processes the input without strict validation, and the malicious change takes effect.
  4. Tampered data is persisted, executed, or relayed downstream — corrupting integrity beyond the original entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Schema-validate and parameterize all inputs
JSON Schema / OpenAPI validation at API edges; parameterized queries / prepared statements for SQL; ORM-level validators on persisted models.
preventive
Sign and verify integrity-critical messages
For commands or state mutations, attach an HMAC or signature so downstream consumers can detect tampering in transit or at rest.
detective
Use append-only / write-once stores for audit-critical data
Where data must not be silently changed, write to an append-only log (e.g., immutable S3 bucket, CloudTrail-like store) and reconcile against the mutable copy.

🔗References

A03:2021 — Injection ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-345 — Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity ↗
Application-layer DDoS
High STRIDE · Denial of Service · Event Bus (queue) CWE-400
▼
CVSS 3.1 6.5
CVSS:3.1/AV:A/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:N/A:H
CVSS 4.0 5.2
CVSS:4.0/AV:A/AC:L/AT:N/PR:N/UI:N/VC:N/VI:N/VA:H/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=7, R=6, E=7, A=5, D=7 · 32/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Event Bus (queue).

📝Description

Attacker floods expensive endpoints (e.g., search, login).

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker identifies a costly operation in Event Bus (regex matching, file generation, expensive query, large allocation).
  2. Issues many concurrent requests targeting that operation, or a single request with pathological input.
  3. Event Bus's resources (CPU, memory, connections, disk) saturate or exhaust.
  4. Legitimate users can no longer reach Event Bus; cascading failure may take down dependents.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply input limits at the edge
Maximum request size, body depth, array length, and string length. Reject early — before parsing or business logic runs.
preventive
Rate-limit per principal and per resource
Token bucket per authenticated user AND per costly endpoint. Adaptive throttling on saturation.
corrective
Set hard timeouts on outbound calls
No call (DB, downstream service, third-party API) should be able to hang the request handler indefinitely. Use circuit breakers and bulkheads.

🔗References

A05:2021 — Security Misconfiguration ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-400 — Uncontrolled Resource Consumption (DoS) ↗
Trust-boundary crossing without strong authn: Orders Service → Event Bus
High STRIDE · Spoofing · Event Bus (queue) CWE-287 ⚡ cross-zone
▼
CVSS 3.1 6.8
CVSS:3.1/AV:A/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:N/A:N
CVSS 4.0 5.8
CVSS:4.0/AV:A/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:H/VI:N/VA:N/SC:H/SI:L/SA:N
DREAD: D=7, R=6, E=5, A=7, D=7 · 32/50
Boundary crossing: Application Tier → Data Tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Orders Service → Event Bus (label: *Orders Service → Event Bus*, protocol: AMQP, auth: mtls, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Application Tier into Data Tier. The receiving component is Event Bus (queue).

📝Description

Flow 'Orders Service → Event Bus' crosses trust boundary 'Application Tier' → 'Data Tier'. Caller identity must be re-verified at the boundary; existing trust does not transit.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker positions themselves on the network path between source and destination, or compromises an upstream component in the source zone.
  2. Crafts requests with forged or replayed identity claims (cookies, tokens, IP-based trust).
  3. Event Bus accepts the request because identity is not re-verified at the boundary, treating the upstream zone as trusted.
  4. Attacker can now perform actions as the impersonated principal across the boundary.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authenticate at the boundary
Require a fresh, audience-bound credential at the receiver. Do not infer identity from network position, source IP, or upstream session.
preventive
Use mutual TLS or signed tokens with audience claims
For service-to-service calls into Event Bus, terminate mTLS at the boundary and reject any caller without a valid client cert. For human-driven flows, use OAuth2 / OIDC with audience and issuer validation.
detective
Log every cross-boundary auth decision
Emit an authentication event (success and failure) tagged with both source and destination zone. Forward to SIEM with retention ≥ 90 days.

🔗References

A07:2021 — Identification and Authentication Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-287 — Improper Authentication ↗
Cross-boundary input not validated: Orders Service → Event Bus
High STRIDE · Tampering · Event Bus (queue) CWE-20 ⚡ cross-zone
▼
CVSS 3.1 6.8
CVSS:3.1/AV:A/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:C/C:N/I:H/A:N
CVSS 4.0 5.8
CVSS:4.0/AV:A/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:N/VI:H/VA:N/SC:L/SI:H/SA:N
DREAD: D=7, R=6, E=5, A=7, D=7 · 32/50
Boundary crossing: Application Tier → Data Tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Orders Service → Event Bus (label: *Orders Service → Event Bus*, protocol: AMQP, auth: mtls, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Application Tier into Data Tier. The receiving component is Event Bus (queue).

📝Description

Data crossing the trust boundary into 'Data Tier' must be treated as untrusted, even if the source is internal. Implicit trust is the most common cause of injection / SSRF / deserialization bugs.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker compromises a less-trusted upstream zone or directly sends malformed input to Event Bus.
  2. Sends payloads with unexpected types, lengths, or special characters (e.g. SQL meta-chars, NUL bytes, control sequences).
  3. Event Bus processes the input under the assumption that the upstream zone has already validated it — but the boundary changes the security context.
  4. Injection, deserialization flaws, or buffer issues trigger; attacker pivots into Event Bus or the zone behind it.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate against an allow-list schema at the boundary
Define an explicit schema (JSON Schema, Protobuf, OpenAPI) for every accepted message at this boundary. Reject anything that doesn't match — type, length, charset, enum values, nested depth.
preventive
Canonicalize before validation
Decode URL-encoding, Unicode normalization (NFC), trim whitespace, and resolve relative paths before validating. Avoid double-decoding bypasses.
preventive
Apply context-specific output encoding
Whatever Event Bus does with the input — SQL: parameterized queries, HTML: contextual escaping, OS commands: avoid shell, use exec arrays, LDAP: escape filter chars.

🔗References

DREAD: D=7, R=6, E=5, A=7, D=8 · 33/50
Boundary crossing: Application Tier → Data Tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Orders Service → Event Bus (label: *Orders Service → Event Bus*, protocol: AMQP, auth: mtls, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Application Tier into Data Tier. The receiving component is Event Bus (queue).

📝Description

Information leaving 'Application Tier' into 'Data Tier' may include data the receiving zone is not authorized to see. Cross-boundary egress is a common data-leak surface.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker observes traffic leaving the source zone, or compromises a component in the destination zone.
  2. The destination zone receives more data than it strictly needs (over-fetching, verbose error responses, full record dumps).
  3. Attacker harvests sensitive fields (PII, secrets, internal IDs) that should never have left the source zone.
  4. Data is exfiltrated, sold, or used to plan a deeper attack on the originating zone.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Enforce minimum-data-needed at the egress point
Whitelist exactly which fields leave the source zone. Strip everything else server-side before serialization.
preventive
Tokenize or redact sensitive fields
Replace PII / PCI / PHI fields with reversible tokens or one-way hashes when the destination zone doesn't need the cleartext value.
detective
Log cross-boundary data flows for review
Sample-log the field set crossing this boundary (not the values) so DLP and privacy reviews can audit what's egressing.

A02:2021 — Cryptographic Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
Unencrypted flow: Catalog Service → Object Store
High STRIDE · Information Disclosure · Object Store (object_storage) CWE-319
▼
CVSS 3.1 7.5
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:N
CVSS 4.0 7.0
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:N/UI:N/VC:H/VI:N/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=8, R=9, E=9, A=5, D=9 · 40/50

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Catalog Service → Object Store (label: *Catalog Service → Object Store*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: none, encrypted: no). The receiving component is Object Store (object_storage).

📝Description

Data flow 'Catalog Service → Object Store' uses HTTPS without encryption.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker gains read access to the network path (sniffing on shared LAN, compromised router, hostile cloud tenant, malicious admin).
  2. Captures cleartext traffic on the HTTPS channel.
  3. Extracts credentials, session tokens, PII, or business secrets from packet captures.
  4. Uses the credentials to impersonate Object Store or the calling component, or sells/leaks the captured data.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Enable TLS 1.3 (or 1.2 with strong ciphers) on the flow
Replace plain HTTPS with its TLS variant. For databases use TLS-enabled drivers; for queues like AMQP/Kafka, configure broker certs and require client TLS.
preventive
Enforce certificate validation
Verify hostname, validate the chain to a known CA, pin certificates or use a private CA for internal services. Disable cipher fallbacks to NULL/EXPORT/RC4.
detective
Scan for cleartext fallbacks
Add a network-policy / NetworkPolicy / security-group rule that drops any traffic on the cleartext port. Alert if it ever fires.

🔗References

A02:2021 — Cryptographic Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-319 — Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information ↗
Unauthenticated flow: Catalog Service → Object Store
High STRIDE · Spoofing · Object Store (object_storage) CWE-306
▼
CVSS 3.1 7.5
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:N
CVSS 4.0 7.0
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:N/UI:N/VC:H/VI:N/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=8, R=9, E=9, A=5, D=8 · 39/50

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Catalog Service → Object Store (label: *Catalog Service → Object Store*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: none, encrypted: no). The receiving component is Object Store (object_storage).

📝Description

Data flow has no authentication mechanism declared.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker discovers the endpoint at Object Store (port scan, leaked config, error messages, or DNS enumeration).
  2. Sends requests directly without any credentials, since the flow does not require authentication.
  3. Object Store processes the request as if it came from a trusted caller and returns data or executes the action.
  4. Attacker enumerates data, modifies records, or chains to other internal services from this entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Require an authentication mechanism on every external-facing flow
Bearer tokens (OAuth2/OIDC) for human-driven calls, mutual TLS or signed JWT for service-to-service. Block requests that arrive without credentials at the gateway, before they reach the application.
preventive
Reject anonymous traffic at the receiver as well
Defense in depth: even if the gateway rule fails, the receiving component should reject any request lacking a valid principal.
detective
Rate-limit unauthenticated probes
Apply a low-tolerance rate limit to requests that lack credentials (5/min per source IP) and alert on sustained failures.

🔗References

CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:N/UI:N/VC:H/VI:N/VA:N/SC:H/SI:L/SA:N
DREAD: D=8, R=7, E=9, A=7, D=9 · 40/50
Boundary crossing: Application Tier → Data Tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Catalog Service → Object Store (label: *Catalog Service → Object Store*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: none, encrypted: no), crossing the trust boundary from Application Tier into Data Tier. The receiving component is Object Store (object_storage).

📝Description

Information leaving 'Application Tier' into 'Data Tier' may include data the receiving zone is not authorized to see. Cross-boundary egress is a common data-leak surface.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker observes traffic leaving the source zone, or compromises a component in the destination zone.
  2. The destination zone receives more data than it strictly needs (over-fetching, verbose error responses, full record dumps).
  3. Attacker harvests sensitive fields (PII, secrets, internal IDs) that should never have left the source zone.
  4. Data is exfiltrated, sold, or used to plan a deeper attack on the originating zone.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Enforce minimum-data-needed at the egress point
Whitelist exactly which fields leave the source zone. Strip everything else server-side before serialization.
preventive
Tokenize or redact sensitive fields
Replace PII / PCI / PHI fields with reversible tokens or one-way hashes when the destination zone doesn't need the cleartext value.
detective
Log cross-boundary data flows for review
Sample-log the field set crossing this boundary (not the values) so DLP and privacy reviews can audit what's egressing.

🔗References

A02:2021 — Cryptographic Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
Data-in-transit modification
High STRIDE · Tampering · Orders DB (database) CWE-345
▼
CVSS 3.1 7.3
CVSS:3.1/AV:A/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:N
CVSS 4.0 5.2
CVSS:4.0/AV:A/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:L/VI:H/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=8, R=6, E=7, A=7, D=7 · 35/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Orders DB (database).

📝Description

Attacker on the network path alters request/response payloads.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches an input vector exposed by Orders DB (request body, query string, message queue payload, persisted state).
  2. Submits malformed input designed to alter the program's logic or stored data (injection, parameter tampering, deserialization gadgets).
  3. Orders DB processes the input without strict validation, and the malicious change takes effect.
  4. Tampered data is persisted, executed, or relayed downstream — corrupting integrity beyond the original entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Schema-validate and parameterize all inputs
JSON Schema / OpenAPI validation at API edges; parameterized queries / prepared statements for SQL; ORM-level validators on persisted models.
preventive
Sign and verify integrity-critical messages
For commands or state mutations, attach an HMAC or signature so downstream consumers can detect tampering in transit or at rest.
detective
Use append-only / write-once stores for audit-critical data
Where data must not be silently changed, write to an append-only log (e.g., immutable S3 bucket, CloudTrail-like store) and reconcile against the mutable copy.

🔗References

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Orders DB (database).

📝Description

Unauthorized changes to runtime config alter security behavior.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches an input vector exposed by Orders DB (request body, query string, message queue payload, persisted state).
  2. Submits malformed input designed to alter the program's logic or stored data (injection, parameter tampering, deserialization gadgets).
  3. Orders DB processes the input without strict validation, and the malicious change takes effect.
  4. Tampered data is persisted, executed, or relayed downstream — corrupting integrity beyond the original entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Schema-validate and parameterize all inputs
JSON Schema / OpenAPI validation at API edges; parameterized queries / prepared statements for SQL; ORM-level validators on persisted models.
preventive
Sign and verify integrity-critical messages
For commands or state mutations, attach an HMAC or signature so downstream consumers can detect tampering in transit or at rest.
detective
Use append-only / write-once stores for audit-critical data
Where data must not be silently changed, write to an append-only log (e.g., immutable S3 bucket, CloudTrail-like store) and reconcile against the mutable copy.

Log tampering
High STRIDE · Repudiation · Orders DB (database) CWE-778
▼
CVSS 3.1 7.3
CVSS:3.1/AV:A/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:N
CVSS 4.0 5.2
CVSS:4.0/AV:A/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:L/VI:H/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=8, R=6, E=7, A=7, D=7 · 35/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Orders DB (database).

📝Description

An attacker with access modifies or deletes audit trails.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. User performs a sensitive action through Orders DB (a transfer, a permission change, a deletion).
  2. Audit logs are absent, incomplete, or modifiable by the same principal who performed the action.
  3. User later denies having performed the action, or an attacker covers their tracks.
  4. Without tamper-evident logs, neither responsible party can be identified — leading to fraud, dispute, or regulatory exposure.

🛡How to mitigate

detective
Emit tamper-evident audit logs
Sign log entries (HMAC chain, hash-linked) so insertion or deletion is detectable. Forward to a separate, append-only system the action's principal cannot administer.
detective
Capture sufficient detail per audit event
Who (authenticated principal, not just session ID), what (specific action and target), when (UTC, monotonic-clock-corroborated), where (source IP, request ID), why (correlated to the upstream business event).
detective
Periodic log integrity verification
Schedule daily integrity checks on the audit log chain. Alert on any gap.

🔗References

A09:2021 — Security Logging and Monitoring Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-778 — Insufficient Logging ↗
Sensitive data exposure in transit
High STRIDE · Information Disclosure · Orders DB (database) CWE-200
▼
CVSS 3.1 5.7
CVSS:3.1/AV:A/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:N
CVSS 4.0 5.2
CVSS:4.0/AV:A/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:H/VI:N/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=8, R=6, E=7, A=7, D=8 · 36/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Orders DB (database).

📝Description

PII, secrets, or tokens transmitted over unencrypted channels.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Orders DB via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For database, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🔗References

Within component Orders DB (database).

📝Description

Stored PII / secrets accessible without proper authorization.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Orders DB via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For database, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.
