A09:2021 — Security Logging and Monitoring Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-778 — Insufficient Logging ↗
Verbose error messages / stack traces
Medium STRIDE · Information Disclosure · Web App (webapp) CWE-200
▼
CVSS 3.1 7.5
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:N
CVSS 4.0 7.0
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:N/UI:N/VC:H/VI:N/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=5, R=4, E=7, A=6, D=8 · 30/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Web App (webapp).

📝Description

Error responses leak implementation details to attackers.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Web App via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For webapp, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🔗References

A02:2021 — Cryptographic Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
Resource exhaustion via unbounded input
Medium STRIDE · Denial of Service · Web App (webapp) CWE-400
▼
CVSS 3.1 7.5
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:N/A:H
CVSS 4.0 7.0
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:N/UI:N/VC:N/VI:N/VA:H/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=5, R=4, E=7, A=6, D=7 · 29/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Web App (webapp).

📝Description

Large payloads or unbounded loops exhaust CPU/memory.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker identifies a costly operation in Web App (regex matching, file generation, expensive query, large allocation).
  2. Issues many concurrent requests targeting that operation, or a single request with pathological input.
  3. Web App's resources (CPU, memory, connections, disk) saturate or exhaust.
  4. Legitimate users can no longer reach Web App; cascading failure may take down dependents.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply input limits at the edge
Maximum request size, body depth, array length, and string length. Reject early — before parsing or business logic runs.
preventive
Rate-limit per principal and per resource
Token bucket per authenticated user AND per costly endpoint. Adaptive throttling on saturation.
corrective
Set hard timeouts on outbound calls
No call (DB, downstream service, third-party API) should be able to hang the request handler indefinitely. Use circuit breakers and bulkheads.

🔗References

A05:2021 — Security Misconfiguration ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-400 — Uncontrolled Resource Consumption (DoS) ↗
Algorithmic complexity attack
Medium STRIDE · Denial of Service · Web App (webapp) CWE-400
▼
CVSS 3.1 7.5
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:N/A:H
CVSS 4.0 7.0
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:N/UI:N/VC:N/VI:N/VA:H/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=5, R=4, E=7, A=6, D=7 · 29/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Web App (webapp).

📝Description

Crafted inputs trigger worst-case algorithm behavior (e.g., regex DoS).

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker identifies a costly operation in Web App (regex matching, file generation, expensive query, large allocation).
  2. Issues many concurrent requests targeting that operation, or a single request with pathological input.
  3. Web App's resources (CPU, memory, connections, disk) saturate or exhaust.
  4. Legitimate users can no longer reach Web App; cascading failure may take down dependents.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply input limits at the edge
Maximum request size, body depth, array length, and string length. Reject early — before parsing or business logic runs.
preventive
Rate-limit per principal and per resource
Token bucket per authenticated user AND per costly endpoint. Adaptive throttling on saturation.
corrective
Set hard timeouts on outbound calls
No call (DB, downstream service, third-party API) should be able to hang the request handler indefinitely. Use circuit breakers and bulkheads.

🔗References

A05:2021 — Security Misconfiguration ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-400 — Uncontrolled Resource Consumption (DoS) ↗
GDPR / CCPA gaps for personal data
High LINDDUN · Non-compliance · API Gateway (api_gateway) CWE-778
▼
CVSS 3.1 0.0
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:N/A:N
CVSS 4.0 5.5
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:N/UI:N/VC:N/VI:N/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=7, R=6, E=9, A=7, D=9 · 38/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component API Gateway (api_gateway).

📝Description

Personal data (PII) triggers GDPR / CCPA duties: lawful basis, DSAR handling, retention limits, and data-residency controls.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker probes API Gateway via reachable inputs.
  2. Identifies the weakness named by this threat (Non-compliance).
  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (api_gateway).
  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply defense-in-depth controls
Layer authentication, authorization, validation, and monitoring around API Gateway. Review applicability of OWASP ASVS controls for api_gateway components.
detective
Add monitoring for the threat indicators
Define detection signals for this threat and forward to your SIEM. Set thresholds based on baseline traffic.

🔗References

  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-778 — Insufficient Logging ↗
Cross-boundary PII transfer: API Gateway → Auth Service
High LINDDUN · Disclosure of information · Auth Service (auth_service) CWE-200 ⚡ cross-zone
▼
CVSS 3.1 7.7
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:N/A:N
CVSS 4.0 5.8
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:H/VI:N/VA:N/SC:H/SI:L/SA:N
DREAD: D=8, R=6, E=7, A=10, D=9 · 40/50
Boundary crossing: Edge → Application Tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow API Gateway → Auth Service (label: *API Gateway → Auth Service*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: mtls, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Edge into Application Tier. The receiving component is Auth Service (auth_service).

📝Description

Personal data crossing trust boundaries triggers data-protection obligations (purpose, consent, residency, processor agreements).

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Auth Service via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For auth_service, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🔗References

DREAD: D=7, R=6, E=9, A=8, D=9 · 39/50

Within component Catalog Service (api).

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Catalog Service via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For api, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply defense-in-depth controls
Layer authentication, authorization, validation, and monitoring around Customer. Review applicability of OWASP ASVS controls for user components.
detective
Add monitoring for the threat indicators
Define detection signals for this threat and forward to your SIEM. Set thresholds based on baseline traffic.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker probes Event Bus via reachable inputs.
  2. Identifies the weakness named by this threat (Non-compliance).

LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-778 — Insufficient Logging ↗
Cross-boundary PII transfer: Orders Service → Event Bus
High LINDDUN · Disclosure of information · Event Bus (queue) CWE-200 ⚡ cross-zone
▼
CVSS 3.1 6.8
CVSS:3.1/AV:A/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:N/A:N
CVSS 4.0 5.8
CVSS:4.0/AV:A/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:H/VI:N/VA:N/SC:H/SI:L/SA:N
DREAD: D=7, R=6, E=5, A=7, D=7 · 32/50
Boundary crossing: Application Tier → Data Tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Orders Service → Event Bus (label: *Orders Service → Event Bus*, protocol: AMQP, auth: mtls, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Application Tier into Data Tier. The receiving component is Event Bus (queue).

📝Description

Personal data crossing trust boundaries triggers data-protection obligations (purpose, consent, residency, processor agreements).

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Event Bus via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For queue, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🔗References

🔗References

LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-778 — Insufficient Logging ↗
Unencrypted flow: Catalog Service → Object Store
High LINDDUN · Disclosure of information · Object Store (object_storage) CWE-319
▼
CVSS 3.1 7.5
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:N
CVSS 4.0 7.0
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:N/UI:N/VC:H/VI:N/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=8, R=9, E=9, A=5, D=9 · 40/50

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Catalog Service → Object Store (label: *Catalog Service → Object Store*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: none, encrypted: no). The receiving component is Object Store (object_storage).

📝Description

Data flow 'Catalog Service → Object Store' uses HTTPS without encryption.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker gains read access to the network path (sniffing on shared LAN, compromised router, hostile cloud tenant, malicious admin).
  2. Captures cleartext traffic on the HTTPS channel.
  3. Extracts credentials, session tokens, PII, or business secrets from packet captures.
  4. Uses the credentials to impersonate Object Store or the calling component, or sells/leaks the captured data.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Enable TLS 1.3 (or 1.2 with strong ciphers) on the flow
Replace plain HTTPS with its TLS variant. For databases use TLS-enabled drivers; for queues like AMQP/Kafka, configure broker certs and require client TLS.
preventive
Enforce certificate validation
Verify hostname, validate the chain to a known CA, pin certificates or use a private CA for internal services. Disable cipher fallbacks to NULL/EXPORT/RC4.
detective
Scan for cleartext fallbacks
Add a network-policy / NetworkPolicy / security-group rule that drops any traffic on the cleartext port. Alert if it ever fires.

🔗References

A02:2021 — Cryptographic Failures ↗LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-319 — Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information ↗
Unauthenticated flow: Catalog Service → Object Store
High LINDDUN · Stage 3 — Application Decomposition · Object Store (object_storage) CWE-306
▼
CVSS 3.1 6.5
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:L/I:L/A:N
CVSS 4.0 5.5
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:N/UI:N/VC:N/VI:N/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=8, R=9, E=9, A=5, D=8 · 39/50

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Catalog Service → Object Store (label: *Catalog Service → Object Store*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: none, encrypted: no). The receiving component is Object Store (object_storage).

📝Description

Data flow has no authentication mechanism declared.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker discovers the endpoint at Object Store (port scan, leaked config, error messages, or DNS enumeration).
  2. Sends requests directly without any credentials, since the flow does not require authentication.
  3. Object Store processes the request as if it came from a trusted caller and returns data or executes the action.
  4. Attacker enumerates data, modifies records, or chains to other internal services from this entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Require an authentication mechanism on every external-facing flow
Bearer tokens (OAuth2/OIDC) for human-driven calls, mutual TLS or signed JWT for service-to-service. Block requests that arrive without credentials at the gateway, before they reach the application.
preventive
Reject anonymous traffic at the receiver as well
Defense in depth: even if the gateway rule fails, the receiving component should reject any request lacking a valid principal.
detective
Rate-limit unauthenticated probes
Apply a low-tolerance rate limit to requests that lack credentials (5/min per source IP) and alert on sustained failures.

🔗References

LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-306 — Missing Authentication for Critical Function ↗

📝Description

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Object Store via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For object_storage, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

Re-identification of anonymized data
High LINDDUN · Identifiability · Orders DB (database) CWE-359
▼
CVSS 3.1 5.7
CVSS:3.1/AV:A/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:N
CVSS 4.0 2.1
CVSS:4.0/AV:A/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:L/VI:N/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=8, R=6, E=7, A=7, D=7 · 35/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Orders DB (database).

📝Description

Quasi-identifiers (zip, DOB, gender) re-identify users in 'anonymous' exports.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker collects records emitted by Orders DB (logs, events, telemetry, public outputs).
  2. Uses quasi-identifiers (timestamps, IPs, device fingerprints, behavioral patterns) to link multiple records to the same individual.
  3. Builds a behavior profile that reveals identity even though no single record contains an explicit identifier.
  4. Privacy violation — re-identification, surveillance, or unauthorized profiling.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply defense-in-depth controls
Layer authentication, authorization, validation, and monitoring around Orders DB. Review applicability of OWASP ASVS controls for database components.
detective
Add monitoring for the threat indicators
Define detection signals for this threat and forward to your SIEM. Set thresholds based on baseline traffic.

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For api, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🔗References

DREAD: D=7, R=6, E=9, A=9, D=9 · 40/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Orders Service (api).

📝Description

Personal data (PII) triggers GDPR / CCPA duties: lawful basis, DSAR handling, retention limits, and data-residency controls.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker probes Orders Service via reachable inputs.
  2. Identifies the weakness named by this threat (Non-compliance).
  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (api).
  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply defense-in-depth controls
Layer authentication, authorization, validation, and monitoring around Orders Service. Review applicability of OWASP ASVS controls for api components.
detective
Add monitoring for the threat indicators
Define detection signals for this threat and forward to your SIEM. Set thresholds based on baseline traffic.

LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-778 — Insufficient Logging ↗
Cross-boundary PII transfer: API Gateway → Orders Service
High LINDDUN · Disclosure of information · Orders Service (api) CWE-200 ⚡ cross-zone
▼
CVSS 3.1 7.7
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:N/A:N
CVSS 4.0 5.8
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:H/VI:N/VA:N/SC:H/SI:L/SA:N
DREAD: D=7, R=6, E=9, A=10, D=9 · 41/50
Boundary crossing: Edge → Application Tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow API Gateway → Orders Service (label: *API Gateway → Orders Service*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: bearer, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Edge into Application Tier. The receiving component is Orders Service (api).

📝Description

Personal data crossing trust boundaries triggers data-protection obligations (purpose, consent, residency, processor agreements).

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Orders Service via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For api, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🔗References

High LINDDUN · Disclosure of information · Sessions Cache (cache) CWE-200 ⚡ cross-zone
▼
CVSS 3.1 6.8
CVSS:3.1/AV:A/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:N/A:N
CVSS 4.0 5.8
CVSS:4.0/AV:A/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:H/VI:N/VA:N/SC:H/SI:L/SA:N
DREAD: D=7, R=6, E=7, A=7, D=7 · 34/50
Boundary crossing: Application Tier → Data Tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Auth Service → Sessions Cache (label: *Auth Service → Sessions Cache*, protocol: TCP, auth: password, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Application Tier into Data Tier. The receiving component is Sessions Cache (cache).

📝Description

Personal data crossing trust boundaries triggers data-protection obligations (purpose, consent, residency, processor agreements).

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Sessions Cache via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For cache, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🔗References

A02:2021 — Cryptographic Failures ↗LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
Re-identification of anonymized data
High LINDDUN · Identifiability · Stripe (external_entity) CWE-359
▼
CVSS 3.1 0.0
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:N/A:N
CVSS 4.0 5.5
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:N/UI:N/VC:N/VI:N/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=7, R=6, E=7, A=5, D=7 · 32/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Stripe (external_entity).

📝Description

Quasi-identifiers (zip, DOB, gender) re-identify users in 'anonymous' exports.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker collects records emitted by Stripe (logs, events, telemetry, public outputs).
  2. Uses quasi-identifiers (timestamps, IPs, device fingerprints, behavioral patterns) to link multiple records to the same individual.
  3. Builds a behavior profile that reveals identity even though no single record contains an explicit identifier.
  4. Privacy violation — re-identification, surveillance, or unauthorized profiling.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply defense-in-depth controls
Layer authentication, authorization, validation, and monitoring around Stripe. Review applicability of OWASP ASVS controls for external_entity components.
detective
Add monitoring for the threat indicators
Define detection signals for this threat and forward to your SIEM. Set thresholds based on baseline traffic.

DREAD: D=7, R=6, E=9, A=8, D=9 · 39/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Web App (webapp).

📝Description

Personal data (PII) triggers GDPR / CCPA duties: lawful basis, DSAR handling, retention limits, and data-residency controls.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker probes Web App via reachable inputs.
  2. Identifies the weakness named by this threat (Non-compliance).
  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (webapp).
  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply defense-in-depth controls
Layer authentication, authorization, validation, and monitoring around Web App. Review applicability of OWASP ASVS controls for webapp components.
detective
Add monitoring for the threat indicators
Define detection signals for this threat and forward to your SIEM. Set thresholds based on baseline traffic.

🔗References

  1. Attacker probes Web App via reachable inputs.

🔗References

LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-778 — Insufficient Logging ↗
Cross-boundary PII transfer: Customer → Web App
High LINDDUN · Disclosure of information · Web App (webapp) CWE-200 ⚡ cross-zone
▼
CVSS 3.1 7.7
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:N/A:N
CVSS 4.0 5.8
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:H/VI:N/VA:N/SC:H/SI:L/SA:N
DREAD: D=7, R=6, E=9, A=10, D=9 · 41/50
Boundary crossing: Public Internet → Edge

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Customer → Web App (label: *Customer → Web App*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: session, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Public Internet into Edge. The receiving component is Web App (webapp).

📝Description

Personal data crossing trust boundaries triggers data-protection obligations (purpose, consent, residency, processor agreements).

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Web App via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For webapp, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🔗References

A02:2021 — Cryptographic Failures ↗LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
Account enumeration via differential responses
Medium LINDDUN · Detectability · Auth Service (auth_service) CWE-359
▼
CVSS 3.1 7.5
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:N
CVSS 4.0 5.5
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:N/UI:N/VC:L/VI:N/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=6, R=4, E=7, A=6, D=8 · 31/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Auth Service (auth_service).

📝Description

Login / signup / password reset reveal whether an account exists.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker probes Auth Service via reachable inputs.
  2. Identifies the weakness named by this threat (Detectability).
  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (auth_service).
  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply defense-in-depth controls
Layer authentication, authorization, validation, and monitoring around Auth Service. Review applicability of OWASP ASVS controls for auth_service components.
detective
Add monitoring for the threat indicators
Define detection signals for this threat and forward to your SIEM. Set thresholds based on baseline traffic.

🔗References

LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-359 — Privacy Violation ↗
Cross-service user linkability
Medium LINDDUN · Linkability · Catalog Service (api) CWE-359
▼
CVSS 3.1 0.0
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:N/A:N
CVSS 4.0 5.5
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:N/UI:N/VC:N/VI:N/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=5, R=4, E=7, A=6, D=7 · 29/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Catalog Service (api).

📝Description

Identifiers (emails, device IDs) let separate datasets be joined to profile a user.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker collects records emitted by Catalog Service (logs, events, telemetry, public outputs).
  2. Uses quasi-identifiers (timestamps, IPs, device fingerprints, behavioral patterns) to link multiple records to the same individual.
  3. Builds a behavior profile that reveals identity even though no single record contains an explicit identifier.
  4. Privacy violation — re-identification, surveillance, or unauthorized profiling.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply defense-in-depth controls
Layer authentication, authorization, validation, and monitoring around Catalog Service. Review applicability of OWASP ASVS controls for api components.
detective
Add monitoring for the threat indicators
Define detection signals for this threat and forward to your SIEM. Set thresholds based on baseline traffic.

  2. Identifies the weakness named by this threat (Unawareness).
  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (webapp).
  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply defense-in-depth controls
Layer authentication, authorization, validation, and monitoring around Web App. Review applicability of OWASP ASVS controls for webapp components.
detective
Add monitoring for the threat indicators
Define detection signals for this threat and forward to your SIEM. Set thresholds based on baseline traffic.

🔗References

LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-359 — Privacy Violation ↗
Forced attribution of sensitive actions
Low LINDDUN · Non-repudiation · Auth Service (auth_service) CWE-778
▼
CVSS 3.1 9.1
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:N
CVSS 4.0 7.0
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:N/UI:N/VC:L/VI:H/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=4, R=2, E=5, A=4, D=5 · 20/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Auth Service (auth_service).

📝Description

User cannot deny a sensitive action even when they should have plausible deniability.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. User performs a sensitive action through Auth Service (a transfer, a permission change, a deletion).
  2. Audit logs are absent, incomplete, or modifiable by the same principal who performed the action.
  3. User later denies having performed the action, or an attacker covers their tracks.
  4. Without tamper-evident logs, neither responsible party can be identified — leading to fraud, dispute, or regulatory exposure.

🛡How to mitigate

detective
Emit tamper-evident audit logs
Sign log entries (HMAC chain, hash-linked) so insertion or deletion is detectable. Forward to a separate, append-only system the action's principal cannot administer.
detective
Capture sufficient detail per audit event
Who (authenticated principal, not just session ID), what (specific action and target), when (UTC, monotonic-clock-corroborated), where (source IP, request ID), why (correlated to the upstream business event).
detective
Periodic log integrity verification
Schedule daily integrity checks on the audit log chain. Alert on any gap.

🔗References

A09:2021 — Security Logging and Monitoring Failures ↗LINDDUN reference ↗CWE-778 — Insufficient Logging ↗

High PASTA · Stage 3 — Application Decomposition · Auth Service (auth_service) CWE-693 ⚡ cross-zone
▼
CVSS 3.1 9.6
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:H/A:N
CVSS 4.0 2.1

DREAD: D=8, R=6, E=7, A=10, D=9 · 40/50
Boundary crossing: Edge → Application Tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow API Gateway → Auth Service (label: *API Gateway → Auth Service*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: mtls, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Edge into Application Tier. The receiving component is Auth Service (auth_service).

📝Description

Decomposition mapped a boundary between 'Edge' and 'Application Tier' but implicit trust persists across it.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker probes Auth Service via reachable inputs.
  2. Identifies the weakness named by this threat (Stage 3 — Application Decomposition).
  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (auth_service).
  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply defense-in-depth controls
Layer authentication, authorization, validation, and monitoring around Auth Service. Review applicability of OWASP ASVS controls for auth_service components.
detective
Add monitoring for the threat indicators
Define detection signals for this threat and forward to your SIEM. Set thresholds based on baseline traffic.

PASTA reference ↗CWE-693 — Protection Mechanism Failure ↗
Unencrypted flow: Catalog Service → Object Store
High PASTA · Disclosure of information · Object Store (object_storage) CWE-319
▼
CVSS 3.1 7.5
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:N
CVSS 4.0 7.0
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:N/UI:N/VC:H/VI:N/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=8, R=9, E=9, A=5, D=9 · 40/50

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Catalog Service → Object Store (label: *Catalog Service → Object Store*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: none, encrypted: no). The receiving component is Object Store (object_storage).

📝Description

Data flow 'Catalog Service → Object Store' uses HTTPS without encryption.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker gains read access to the network path (sniffing on shared LAN, compromised router, hostile cloud tenant, malicious admin).
  2. Captures cleartext traffic on the HTTPS channel.
  3. Extracts credentials, session tokens, PII, or business secrets from packet captures.
  4. Uses the credentials to impersonate Object Store or the calling component, or sells/leaks the captured data.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Enable TLS 1.3 (or 1.2 with strong ciphers) on the flow
Replace plain HTTPS with its TLS variant. For databases use TLS-enabled drivers; for queues like AMQP/Kafka, configure broker certs and require client TLS.
preventive
Enforce certificate validation
Verify hostname, validate the chain to a known CA, pin certificates or use a private CA for internal services. Disable cipher fallbacks to NULL/EXPORT/RC4.
detective
Scan for cleartext fallbacks
Add a network-policy / NetworkPolicy / security-group rule that drops any traffic on the cleartext port. Alert if it ever fires.

🔗References

A02:2021 — Cryptographic Failures ↗PASTA reference ↗CWE-319 — Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information ↗
Unauthenticated flow: Catalog Service → Object Store
High PASTA · Stage 3 — Application Decomposition · Object Store (object_storage) CWE-306
▼
CVSS 3.1 6.5
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:L/I:L/A:N
CVSS 4.0 5.5
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:N/UI:N/VC:N/VI:N/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=8, R=9, E=9, A=5, D=8 · 39/50

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Catalog Service → Object Store (label: *Catalog Service → Object Store*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: none, encrypted: no). The receiving component is Object Store (object_storage).

📝Description

Data flow has no authentication mechanism declared.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker discovers the endpoint at Object Store (port scan, leaked config, error messages, or DNS enumeration).
  2. Sends requests directly without any credentials, since the flow does not require authentication.
  3. Object Store processes the request as if it came from a trusted caller and returns data or executes the action.
  4. Attacker enumerates data, modifies records, or chains to other internal services from this entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Require an authentication mechanism on every external-facing flow
Bearer tokens (OAuth2/OIDC) for human-driven calls, mutual TLS or signed JWT for service-to-service. Block requests that arrive without credentials at the gateway, before they reach the application.
preventive
Reject anonymous traffic at the receiver as well
Defense in depth: even if the gateway rule fails, the receiving component should reject any request lacking a valid principal.
detective
Rate-limit unauthenticated probes
Apply a low-tolerance rate limit to requests that lack credentials (5/min per source IP) and alert on sustained failures.

🔗References

PASTA reference ↗CWE-306 — Missing Authentication for Critical Function ↗

PASTA reference ↗CWE-693 — Protection Mechanism Failure ↗
Manual step — map business impact & asset owners
Info PASTA · Stage 1 — Business Objectives · API Gateway (api_gateway) CWE-693
▼
CVSS 3.1 6.5
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:L/I:L/A:N
CVSS 4.0 5.5
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:N/UI:N/VC:N/VI:N/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=2, R=1, E=4, A=2, D=4 · 13/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component API Gateway (api_gateway).

📝Description

PASTA Stage 1 requires defining business objectives and impact tiers. The tool does NOT determine this automatically: assign an owner and a business-impact tier to each component, and record revenue/regulatory exposure.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker probes API Gateway via reachable inputs.
  2. Identifies the weakness named by this threat (Stage 1 — Business Objectives).
  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (api_gateway).
  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply defense-in-depth controls
Layer authentication, authorization, validation, and monitoring around API Gateway. Review applicability of OWASP ASVS controls for api_gateway components.
detective
Add monitoring for the threat indicators
Define detection signals for this threat and forward to your SIEM. Set thresholds based on baseline traffic.

🔗References

PASTA reference ↗CWE-693 — Protection Mechanism Failure ↗
Manual step — enumerate dependencies & run SCA
Info PASTA · Stage 2 — Technical Scope · API Gateway (api_gateway) CWE-693
▼
CVSS 3.1 6.5
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:L/I:L/A:N
CVSS 4.0 5.5
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:N/UI:N/VC:N/VI:N/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=2, R=1, E=4, A=2, D=4 · 13/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component API Gateway (api_gateway).

📝Description

PASTA Stage 2 requires a dependency/SBOM inventory and CVE analysis. The tool does NOT scan dependencies: run SCA (e.g. dependabot/trivy) and attach the SBOM to complete this stage.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker probes API Gateway via reachable inputs.
  2. Identifies the weakness named by this threat (Stage 2 — Technical Scope).
  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (api_gateway).
  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply defense-in-depth controls
Layer authentication, authorization, validation, and monitoring around API Gateway. Review applicability of OWASP ASVS controls for api_gateway components.
detective
Add monitoring for the threat indicators
Define detection signals for this threat and forward to your SIEM. Set thresholds based on baseline traffic.

📍Where the threat exists

Within component API Gateway (api_gateway).

📝Description

PASTA Stage 4 requires profiling plausible adversaries and their techniques. The tool does NOT infer threat actors: map actor tiers (script-kiddie → nation-state) and relevant MITRE ATT&CK TTPs to your surfaces.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker probes API Gateway via reachable inputs.
  2. Identifies the weakness named by this threat (Stage 4 — Threat Analysis).
  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (api_gateway).
  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

DREAD: D=2, R=1, E=4, A=2, D=4 · 13/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component API Gateway (api_gateway).

📝Description

PASTA Stage 6 requires constructing attack trees. The tool does NOT generate attack trees: model plausible kill-chains (e.g. phish → token theft → admin) for the highest-risk flows and rank them by likelihood × impact.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker probes API Gateway via reachable inputs.
  2. Identifies the weakness named by this threat (Stage 6 — Attack Modeling).
  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (api_gateway).
  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

📍Where the threat exists

Within component API Gateway (api_gateway).

📝Description

PASTA Stage 7 quantifies and prioritizes risk. The tool provides severity and DREAD scores per threat as inputs, but does NOT record risk decisions: track each risk to an explicit accept/mitigate/transfer/avoid decision with an owner.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker probes API Gateway via reachable inputs.
  2. Identifies the weakness named by this threat (Stage 7 — Risk & Impact Analysis).
  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (api_gateway).
  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Auth Service (auth_service).

📝Description

PASTA Stage 1 requires defining business objectives and impact tiers. The tool does NOT determine this automatically: assign an owner and a business-impact tier to each component, and record revenue/regulatory exposure.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker probes Auth Service via reachable inputs.
  2. Identifies the weakness named by this threat (Stage 1 — Business Objectives).
  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (auth_service).
  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

Layer authentication, authorization, validation, and monitoring around Customer. Review applicability of OWASP ASVS controls for user components.
detective
Add monitoring for the threat indicators
Define detection signals for this threat and forward to your SIEM. Set thresholds based on baseline traffic.

🔗References

PASTA reference ↗CWE-693 — Protection Mechanism Failure ↗
Manual step — enumerate dependencies & run SCA
Info PASTA · Stage 2 — Technical Scope · Customer (user) CWE-693
▼
CVSS 3.1 6.5
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:L/I:L/A:N
CVSS 4.0 5.5
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:N/UI:N/VC:N/VI:N/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=2, R=1, E=4, A=1, D=4 · 12/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Customer (user).

📝Description

PASTA Stage 2 requires a dependency/SBOM inventory and CVE analysis. The tool does NOT scan dependencies: run SCA (e.g. dependabot/trivy) and attach the SBOM to complete this stage.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker probes Customer via reachable inputs.
  2. Identifies the weakness named by this threat (Stage 2 — Technical Scope).
  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (user).
  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply defense-in-depth controls
Layer authentication, authorization, validation, and monitoring around Customer. Review applicability of OWASP ASVS controls for user components.
detective
Add monitoring for the threat indicators
Define detection signals for this threat and forward to your SIEM. Set thresholds based on baseline traffic.

DREAD: D=2, R=1, E=4, A=1, D=4 · 12/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Customer (user).

📝Description

PASTA Stage 4 requires profiling plausible adversaries and their techniques. The tool does NOT infer threat actors: map actor tiers (script-kiddie → nation-state) and relevant MITRE ATT&CK TTPs to your surfaces.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker probes Customer via reachable inputs.
  2. Identifies the weakness named by this threat (Stage 4 — Threat Analysis).
  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (user).
  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

▼
CVSS 3.1 6.5
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:L/I:L/A:N

DREAD: D=2, R=1, E=4, A=1, D=4 · 12/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Customer (user).

📝Description

PASTA Stage 6 requires constructing attack trees. The tool does NOT generate attack trees: model plausible kill-chains (e.g. phish → token theft → admin) for the highest-risk flows and rank them by likelihood × impact.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker probes Customer via reachable inputs.
  2. Identifies the weakness named by this threat (Stage 6 — Attack Modeling).
  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (user).
  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Customer (user).

📝Description

PASTA Stage 7 quantifies and prioritizes risk. The tool provides severity and DREAD scores per threat as inputs, but does NOT record risk decisions: track each risk to an explicit accept/mitigate/transfer/avoid decision with an owner.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker probes Customer via reachable inputs.
  2. Identifies the weakness named by this threat (Stage 7 — Risk & Impact Analysis).
  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (user).
  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Event Bus (queue).

📝Description

PASTA Stage 1 requires defining business objectives and impact tiers. The tool does NOT determine this automatically: assign an owner and a business-impact tier to each component, and record revenue/regulatory exposure.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker probes Event Bus via reachable inputs.
  2. Identifies the weakness named by this threat (Stage 1 — Business Objectives).
  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (queue).
  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

Manual step — build attack trees
Info PASTA · Stage 6 — Attack Modeling · Orders DB (database) CWE-200
▼
CVSS 3.1 6.3
CVSS:3.1/AV:A/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:L/A:N
CVSS 4.0 2.1
CVSS:4.0/AV:A/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:L/VI:N/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=3, R=1, E=2, A=2, D=2 · 10/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Orders DB (database).

📝Description

PASTA Stage 6 requires constructing attack trees. The tool does NOT generate attack trees: model plausible kill-chains (e.g. phish → token theft → admin) for the highest-risk flows and rank them by likelihood × impact.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker probes Orders DB via reachable inputs.
  2. Identifies the weakness named by this threat (Stage 6 — Attack Modeling).
  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (database).
  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply defense-in-depth controls
Layer authentication, authorization, validation, and monitoring around Orders DB. Review applicability of OWASP ASVS controls for database components.
detective
Add monitoring for the threat indicators
Define detection signals for this threat and forward to your SIEM. Set thresholds based on baseline traffic.

CVSS 3.1 5.4
CVSS:3.1/AV:A/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:L/I:L/A:N
CVSS 4.0 2.1
CVSS:4.0/AV:A/AC:L/AT:N/PR:N/UI:N/VC:N/VI:N/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=2, R=1, E=2, A=1, D=2 · 8/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Sessions Cache (cache).

📝Description

PASTA Stage 7 quantifies and prioritizes risk. The tool provides severity and DREAD scores per threat as inputs, but does NOT record risk decisions: track each risk to an explicit accept/mitigate/transfer/avoid decision with an owner.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker probes Sessions Cache via reachable inputs.
  2. Identifies the weakness named by this threat (Stage 7 — Risk & Impact Analysis).
  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (cache).
  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply defense-in-depth controls
Layer authentication, authorization, validation, and monitoring around Sessions Cache. Review applicability of OWASP ASVS controls for cache components.
detective
Add monitoring for the threat indicators
Define detection signals for this threat and forward to your SIEM. Set thresholds based on baseline traffic.

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Stripe (external_entity).

📝Description

PASTA Stage 1 requires defining business objectives and impact tiers. The tool does NOT determine this automatically: assign an owner and a business-impact tier to each component, and record revenue/regulatory exposure.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker probes Stripe via reachable inputs.
  2. Identifies the weakness named by this threat (Stage 1 — Business Objectives).
  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (external_entity).
  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

Within component Stripe (external_entity).

📝Description

PASTA Stage 4 requires profiling plausible adversaries and their techniques. The tool does NOT infer threat actors: map actor tiers (script-kiddie → nation-state) and relevant MITRE ATT&CK TTPs to your surfaces.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker probes Stripe via reachable inputs.
  2. Identifies the weakness named by this threat (Stage 4 — Threat Analysis).
  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (external_entity).
  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Stripe (external_entity).

📝Description

PASTA Stage 6 requires constructing attack trees. The tool does NOT generate attack trees: model plausible kill-chains (e.g. phish → token theft → admin) for the highest-risk flows and rank them by likelihood × impact.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker probes Stripe via reachable inputs.
  2. Identifies the weakness named by this threat (Stage 6 — Attack Modeling).
  3. Crafts an exploit specific to the affected component type (external_entity).
  4. Successful exploitation leads to the impact described — loss of confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy.
